coredata.dc:title: Synergistic coupling of FeNi3 alloy with graphene carbon dots for advanced oxygen evolution reaction electrocatalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhao
Xu, Xianli
Lu, Xinhua
He, Changchun
Huang, Jingjing
Sun, Wei
Tian, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Realizing the simultaneous morphology and electrical conductivity tuning of non-noble metal nanocatalysts is urgently desired for promoting their intrinsic activity toward oxygen evolution reaction (OER), while it is still challenging. Herein, we have demonstrated that the morphology and conductivity of FeNi3 alloy can be finely tailored via introducing the graphene carbon dots (GCDs). Benefitting from the enlarged active areas, significantly improved electrical conductivity, and strong synergistic coupling effect, the optimized FeNi3@GCDs-10 shows extraordinary electrocatalytic performance towards OER by delivering a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with the overpotential of 238 mV, as well as small Tafel slope of 48.7 mV dec-1. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that Fe is the dominated active sites for boosting water dissociation. In addition, the incorporation of GCDs can also strength the adsorption of *O and lower the energy barrier for the transformation from *OH to *O, thereby optimizing the free energy of *O and greatly promoting the OER activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FeNi3 alloy
Graphene carbon dots
Fe sites
Synergistic effect
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GCDs (inset is corresponding HRTEM), (b) FeNi3, (c-d) and FeNi3@GCDs-10. (e) HRTEM image and (f) SAED pattern of FeNi3@GCDs-10. (g) STEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping images of Fe, Ni, and C in FeNi3@GCDs-10. (h) The compositional line profile in Fig. 1g and the (i) EDS spectrum of FeNi3@GCDs-10.
XPS spectra of FeNi3@GCDs-10. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) Fe 2p, (c) Ni 2p, (d) C 1 s, and (e) O 1 s.

(a) LSV polarization curves of FeNi3, GCDs, RuO2, FeNi3@GCDs-8 FeNi3@GCDs-10, and FeNi3@GCDs-12. (b) Histograms of the overpotentials of different catalysts at 10 mA cm−2. (c) Tafel plots of FeNi3, FeNi3@GCDs-8 FeNi3@GCDs-10, and FeNi3@GCDs-12. (d) Current density as a function of the scan rate for the different electrodes. (e) Nyquist plots of FeNi3, FeNi3@GCDs-8 FeNi3@GCDs-10, and FeNi3@GCDs-12 at 0.5 V. (f) LSV polarization curves of FeNi3@GCDs-10 before and after 3000 CV cycles.

(a) Schematically presenting the electrochemical overall water splitting based on FeNi3@GCDs-10//Pt/C couple. (b) LSV polarization curves of FeNi3@GCDs-10//Pt/C and RuO2//Pt/C toward overall water splitting. (c) CP curve of FeNi3@GCDs-10//Pt/C couple for water electrolysis at 10 mA cm−2.
Evolutions of XPS spectrum of (a) Ni 2p and (b) Fe 2p.

The reaction paths on different models and the corresponding free energies. (a) at the set potential of 0 V, (b) at the set potential of 1.23 V. (c) The PDOS of Fe in different environments. (d) Differential charge density of C-Ni3Fe model (Yellow region represents the enhanced electron density; Blue represents the decreased electron region).
The proposed enhanced mechanism of FeNi3@GCDs.
Schematic illustration of preparation of FeNi3@GCDs.
Schematic illustration for the various morphology formation of FeNi3 by adding GCDs with different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Synergistic coupling of FeNi3 alloy with graphene carbon dots for advanced oxygen evolution reaction electrocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou 221018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou 221018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Realizing the simultaneous morphology and electrical conductivity tuning of non-noble metal nanocatalysts is urgently desired for promoting their intrinsic activity toward oxygen evolution reaction (OER), while it is still challenging. Herein, we have demonstrated that the morphology and conductivity of FeNi3 alloy can be finely tailored via introducing the graphene carbon dots (GCDs). Benefitting from the enlarged active areas, significantly improved electrical conductivity, and strong synergistic coupling effect, the optimized FeNi3@GCDs-10 shows extraordinary electrocatalytic performance towards OER by delivering a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with the overpotential of 238 mV, as well as small Tafel slope of 48.7 mV dec-1. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that Fe is the dominated active sites for boosting water dissociation. In addition, the incorporation of GCDs can also strength the adsorption of *O and lower the energy barrier for the transformation from *OH to *O, thereby optimizing the free energy of *O and greatly promoting the OER activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrolytic water splitting is an attractive path for realizing the large-scale production of renewable and clean hydrogen [1,2]. However, anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) seems to be the bottleneck in this process, as it is kinetically sluggish and demands two steps of OH bonding breaking and subsequent OO bond formation, involving of four electron transfer process [3,4]. Therefore, a substantial overpotential is inevitable for OER to achieve a desirable current. To address this issue, introducing appropriate electrocatalysts is urgently sought. In general, noble metal compounds, such as IrO2 and RuO2 are widely considered as the state-of-the-art OER electrocatalysts because of their inherently high intrinsic activity [5]. However, unfortunately, the high cost and unsatisfied electrochemical stability have greatly limited their practical applications [6]. Accordingly, exploring cost-effective and highly stable materials to substitute noble metal nanocatalysts is highly imperative. In the past decade, intensive endeavors have been made to the exploration of advanced OER electrocatalysts [7], and some earth-abundant first row (3d) transition metal compounds (TMCs) have been successfully developed as promising catalysts for boosting OER electrocatalysis [8–10]. Although they have been demonstrated to possess great advantages for OER electrocatalysis, a huge gap between their OER activity and industrial standard is still existed [11]. The most important reason is ascribed to the relatively low intrinsic activity, finite surface active sites, and unsatisfactory electrical conductivity [12].

Recently, researchers have disclosed that the OER performance of TMCs can be significantly improved by rationally tuning morphology and electrical conductivity [13,14]. Besides the morphology and conductivity engineering, constructing multi-component catalysts is also another favorable approach for facilitating OER electrocatalysis, as the synergistic effect between different components can also promote the electrocatalytic performance of OER catalysts to a higher level [15–17]. Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimension nanomaterial, has appealed lots of attention due to their numerous attractive properties, such as easily prepared, environment-friendly, good biocompatibility, and excellent optical property [18,19]. Moreover, benefitting from the abundant functional groups (–OH, –CO-, –COOH) in their surface, CDs could easily reunite with TMCs to generate nanocomposites [20]. Some reports have demonstrated that CDs play a key role in increasing OER property through regulating morphology and conductivity of composites. For example, Wu et al. optimized the morphology of CoP by changing the contents of CDs in the reaction process [21]. Lu and his colleagues have improved the electrical conductivity of Ru with the aid of CDs [22]. Our group have regulated the morphology of MnO2 and γ-MnOOH with assistance of CDs to significantly enhance their OER performance [23,24]. Although substantial efforts have been made, the simultaneous regulation of morphology and electrical conductivity in a catalyst has been rarely reported [25]. Besides, the regulation mechanism of morphology induced by CDs and underlying causes for improving the OER performance are still vague.

Inspired by this, we herein present the simultaneous tuning of morphology and electrical conductivity of FeNi3 alloy by introducing graphene carbon dots (GCDs) and provide an insight into the role that GCDs play in improving OER performance. It is interesting that the incorporation of GCDs can not only effectively regulate the morphology of the FeNi3 alloy, but also largely promote the electrical conductivity, thereby greatly contributing to the electrocatalytic OER performance. As a consequence, the composition- and morphology-optimized FeNi3@GCDs-10 showed remarkably high OER performance, which demanded an overpotential of 238 mV at 10 mA cm−2, as well as outstanding electrochemical stability. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and theoretical calculations revealed that Fe sites were the real active sites for boosting OER electrocatalysis, and the introduction of GCDs strengthened the adsorption of *O and lowered the energy barrier for formation of *O, thereby optimizing the free energy of *O. Moreover, it was also disclosed that the strong synergistic effect of GCDs and FeNi3 alloy also contributed to excellent OER performance.

First, a mixture of 0.5 g NaNO3 and 1.0 g graphite powder was poured into 24 mL concentrated H2SO4 under stirring for 30 min in an ice-bath. Then, 3 g KMnO4 were added into above solution and keeping stirring for another 30 min at room temperature. After that, the mixture was transferred into water-bath at 35 °C for stirring 60 min, then 46 mL DI water was slowly poured into the mixture. Subsequently, 140 mL DI water and 10 mL 30% H2O2 were successively added into the mixture until the temperature of reaction system reached to 98 °C. Finally, the obtained solution was successively washed with 5% HCl, BaCl2 solution, DI water, and dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h.

10 g GO were poured into the mixture of 450 mL con. H2SO4 and 150 mL con. HNO3 under 120 °C for 24 h. Then, 1200 mL DI water was added after the mixture was cooled into room temperature. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed with an intercepted molecular weight of 500 Da for five days. Finally, GCDs were obtained after freeze-drying for 12 h.

A mixture containing 260 mg NiCl2·6H2O, 210 mg FeCl3·6H2O, and 10 mg GCDs was poured into 12 mL DI water and keeping ultrasound for 30 min. Then, 3.8 mL N2H4·H2O solution containing 500 mg NaOH was slowly added into the mixture. Stirring for 20 min, the solution was transferred into 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept reacting for 2 h at 170 °C. The final FeNi3@GCDs-10 were obtained by washing with DI water and ethanol for several times. FeNi3, FeNi3@GCDs-8, and FeNi3@GCDs-12 were obtained by the similar method except for varying the contenting of GCDs from 10 mg to 0 mg, 8 mg, and 12 mg, respectively.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on CHI-760E electrochemical workstation by using a standard three-electrode system, which employed a modified glass carbon electrode with samples, Ag/AgCl, and carbon rod as the working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) is measured with an voltage ranging 0 V to 0.7 V at the scan rate of 5 mV·s−1. iR drop was compensated at 95% for measurements. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) is calculated by using cyclic voltammetry (CV) in a non-faradaic region at different scanning rates from 10 to 400 mV·s−1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is tested in a range of 1 Hz-1000 kHz with a sinusoidal perturbation of 0.5 mA.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is operated via a Rigaku UIV powder diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The morphologies of different samples are tested using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is recorded on JEOL JPS-9010 MC spectrometer to explore the composition and valence state of the sample.

All DFT calculations were performed using Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [26]. The projector augmented wave (PAW) pseudopotential [27] with the PBE generalized gradient approximation (GGA) exchange correlation function [28] was utilized in the computations. The cutoff energy of the plane waves basis set was 500 eV and a Monkhorst-Pack mesh of 3 × 3 × 1 was used in K-sampling (9 × 9 × 1 was used in calculating the DOS). All structures were spin polarized and all atoms were fully relaxed with the energy convergence tolerance of 10–5 eV per atom, and the final force on each atom was < 0.05 eV Å−1.

All periodic slabs have a vacuum layer of at least 15 Å. In the calculation, the model constructed four layers of nickel and iron. Nickel and iron at the bottom are fixed at their optimized bulk-truncated positions during geometry optimization, and the rest of atoms could relax. On the surface of the model, we added graphite to simulate carbon coating on the surface of the material. The adsorption energy of reaction intermediates, can be computed using the following Equation (1):
(1)
Δ
G
ads
=
E
ads
- E ∗ + Δ
E
ZPE
- T Δ S

Where ads = (OH*, O*, OOH*), and (Eads-E*) is the binding energy, ΔEZPE is the zero-point energy change, ΔS is the entropy change. In this work, the values of ΔEZPE and ΔS were obtained by vibration frequency calculation.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the dendrite-like FeNi3@GCDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal approach by mixing NiCl2·6H2O and FeCl3·6H2O with previously synthesized the GCDs and employing N2H4 as reductants. The morphologies of the as-obtained GCDs, FeNi3 alloys, and FeNi3@GCDs-10 were characterized via TEM. As shown in Fig. 1
a, ultrasmall GCDs with an average diameter of 5 nm were homogeneously dispersed. The HRTEM image shows the lattice spacing of 0.246 nm, which is consistent with the (100) plane of carbon dots, further demonstrating the GCDs were successful constructed [29]. Fig. 1 b shows the TEM image of the FeNi3 alloy that prepared in absence of GCDs, it is clearly observed that the FeNi3 alloys are featured with sphere-like nanostructures with rough surface (Fig. 1 c). Interestingly, when 10 mg GCDs were added, the morphology of the nanohybrids is apparently changed to dendrite-like structure (Fig. 1 d), which may be favorable for affording more accessible surface active sites for reactants and intermediated species [30,31].

From the HRTEM image in Fig. 1 e, the lattice fringes with distance of 0.206 nm and 0.243 nm were assigned to the (111) plane of FeNi3 and (010) plane of GCDs [32,33], respectively, confirming the successful fabrication of FeNi3@GCDs with rich heterointerfaces, which may be conductive to the electron transfer during electrochemical reactions [34]. The bright ring composed of discrete spots in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of FeNi3@GCDS-10 in Fig. 1 f can well match with the (111) plane of FeNi3 and (100) plane of GCDs. The high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and corresponding elemental mapping images (Fig. 1 g) of FeNi3@GCDs-10 displayed that the elements of C, Fe, and Ni were uniformly distributed throughout the dendrite-like nanomaterials. The line-scan profiles and EDS result for FeNi3@GCDs-10 suggested the uniform existence of C, Fe and Ni in the surface of FeNi3@GCDs-10 (Fig. 1 h, i). In addition to the morphology characterizations, we have also conducted the XRD tests to study the crystal structure of the nanohybrids. As seen in Fig. S1, the XRD diffraction peaks of the FeNi3 alloy and FeNi3@GCDs can be well indexed to the FeNi3 alloy (PDF#38–0419), evidencing the formation of alloy phase in these nanomaterials [35], while no obvious peaks for GCDs may due to its low content in the nanohybrids [36].

To elucidate the role of the GCDs in the evolution process of the morphology, GCDs with varying contents were added during the sonication process and their morphologies were characterized by TEM techniques. Fig. 1 b showed a spherical morphology in the absence of GCDs, which was similar to previous report [37]. When the content of GCDs is increased to 8 mg, a similar orbicular morphology can be observed with larger radius compared with the original one (Fig. S2a). When further increasing the content of GCDs to 10 mg, the dendritic morphology could be obviously found (Fig. 1 c). However, the contents of GCDs were further increased, the dendrite-like structure diminished and the spherical morphology appeared again (Fig. S2b). Based on these results, a supposed formation mechanism for FeNi3@GCDs samples with different morphologies is illustrated in Scheme 2 . Without GCDs, Fe/Ni precursor crystal nucleus grew to generate a spherical morphology in the hydrothermal process [38]. Interestingly, after the introduction of GCDs, the GCDs could easily anchored into the surface of FeNi3 nucleus due to enriched functional groups on the surface of GCDs [39]. When appropriate contents of GCDs were added, some GCDs were used to anchor the spherical surface of FeNi3 nucleus, while residual GCDs would restrain the formation of spherical morphology and caused the nucleus to grow into a dart-like structure, forming the final dendritic structure [40]. However, the introduction of excessive GCDs would boost the FeNi3 nucleus to grow into rod-like structure and stack into a similar morphology with original one [41].

The obtained FeNi3@GCDs-10 is further characterized by XPS in Fig. 2 , which uncovers the existence of Fe, Ni, O, and N in this composite ( Fig. 2 a). In Fig. 2 b, the peaks appear around 704.9 eV, 707.5 eV, and 712.7 eV are ascribed to the Ni Auger peak, metallic Fe0, and Fe2+ 2p3/2 peaks [42], respectively, while the Fe3+ is also proved by the corresponding peaks with the binding energy of 718.9 eV. Moreover, the broad peak around 725.1 eV can also assigned to the Fe 2p1/2 [43]. As shown in Fig. 2 c, the deconvoluted Ni 2p spectrum contains the peak of Ni0 from 852.4 eV, and the Ni2+ is established by two peaks located at around 855.7 and 873.4 eV, while the peaks at 861.4 and 879.6 eV are assigned to the satellite peaks of Ni2+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 [44], respectively. The peak of C 1 s spectrum can be divided into four peaks at 284.7, 285.5, 286.7 and 288.7 eV, which are corresponding to CC sp2, CC sp3, CO, and OC-O [45], respectively (Fig. 2 d). In Fig. 2 e, the fine peak of O 1 s contain two peaks around 530.8 and 531.4 eV, which can be ascribed to the M−O and M−OH [46], respectively.

In consideration of the distinctive physiochemical properties of the FeNi3@GCDs, we herein investigated their electrocatalytic OER performance by benchmarking the FeNi3 alloy, GCDs, and commercial RuO2. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the FeNi3@GCDs displayed apparently larger current density as compared to the FeNi3 alloy, GCDs, and commercial RuO2. However, the FeNi3/GCDs mixture that obtained by physically mixing FeNi3 and GCDs under ultrasonicating for 0.5 h, only showed the larger current density than GCDs at the same overpotential, implying the remarkably synergistic effect between FeNi3 and GCDs. To reach to 10 mA cm−2, the optimal FeNi3@GCDs-10 requires an ultralow overpotential of merely 238 mV (Fig. 3 b), being much lower than those of FeNi3 alloy (339 mV), FeNi3/GCDs mixture (380 mV), commercial RuO2 (324 mV), and some recently reported OER electrocatalysts (Table S1). In addition, both the FeNi3@GCDs-8 and FeNi3@GCDs-12 also demanded the low overpotentials of 308 mV and 310 mV at 10 mA cm−2, respectively, implying the outstanding OER activity of FeNi3@GCDs [47]. To reveal the kinetics of the FeNi3@GCDs, we have also calculated the Tafel slopes of different electrocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 3 c, the FeNi3@GCDs-10 exhibited the Tafel slope of 48.70 mV dec-1, which was also much smaller than that of FeNi3 alloy (55.41 mV dec-1). And the other two FeNi3@GCDs catalysts could also show small Tafel slope, which indicated the fast reaction kinetics of the FeNi3@GCDs toward OER electrocatalysis [48]. Moreover, the improved electrical conductivity is also evidenced by EIS spectrum. As seen in Fig. 3 d and Fig. S3, the FeNi3@GCDs showed apparently smaller EIS diameter as compared to the FeNi3 alloy and FeNi3/GCDs mixture mixture, suggesting that the synergetic effect between FeNi3 and GCDs could largely promote the electrical conductivity of integrated FeNi3@GCDs to boost the charge transfer [49], which is consistent with the Tafel slope analysis. In addition to the OER activity and reaction kinetics, the electrochemical activity surface area (ECSA) is also crucial to the OER performance. In general, a catalyst with a large ECSA value would expose more accessible surface active sites for reactants and intermediates. Therefore, the ECSA plays a crucial role in determining the OER performance. In this case, we herein calculated the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) by CV scanning in the non-faradaic range at different scan rates to roughly compare the number of active sites, as the Cdl is positively correlated with the ECSA. From Fig. 3 e, it is clearly observed that the FeNi3@GCDs-10 possesses a much larger Cdl than FeNi3 alloy, suggesting the more accessible active sites of FeNi3@GCDs-10 for electrocatalytic OER [50]. In light of the high OER activity and good electron transfer capability, we have also investigated the electrochemical stability of the FeNi3@GCDs-10 by comparing the LSV curves before and after 3000 potential cycles. As shown in Fig. 3 f, the LSV polarization curve of the FeNi3@GCDs-10 after 3000 potential cycles showed negligible variation as compared to the initial LSV curve, implying the outstanding OER durability [51]. For comparation, the stability of pristine FeNi3 alloy was also tested. As shown in Fig. S4a, the current of FeNi3 after 500 potential CV cycles sharply decreased, indicating the addition of GCDs can effectively facilitate the stability of nanohybrid. Different from the FeNi3@GCDs, the CP test suggested that the potential for FeNi3/GCDs to deliver 10 mA cm−2 was significantly increased for only 1 h, confirming that the synergistic effect between FeNi3 and GCDs could improve the stability [52].

In light of the remarkably high OER activity, we herein assembled the FeNi3@GCDs-10 with commercial Pt/C to drive overall water splitting in a two-electrode system (Fig. 4
a). The FeNi3@GCDs-10||Pt/C couple can exhibit high electrocatalytic water splitting performance, affording a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with a cell voltage of only 1.58 V (Fig. 4 b), which is comparable to the state-of-the-art RuO2//Pt/C system [53]. This performance is also much superior to recently reported electrocatalysts (Table S1). In particular, the chronoamperometry (CP) test showed negligible performance decay to deliver 10 mA cm−2 for more than 55 h (Fig. 4 c), confirming the outstanding lone-term electrochemical stability and appreciable application for hydrogen production via electrochemical water splitting [54].

The changes of surface elements chemical state before and after stability test are important for exploring the origin of stability and catalytic mechanism. In Fig. 5
a, the intensity of the minor peaks from Ni0 components decrease while the major peak mainly from Ni2+ 2p3/2 species shift to corresponding satellite peak. and the peak of Ni2+ 2p1/2 also shows the same changes. However, the Ni3+, which is usually regarded as the real active center, is not observed after stability test, implying the Ni maybe not the active sites [55]. From Fig. 5 b, we can easily observe that the region of Fe3+ 2p3/2 is remarkably increased when compared with original FeNi3@GCDs-10, which may be ascribed to the formation of substantial Fe(II) oxyhydroxides [56]. The metal oxyhydroxides were regarded as active species during OER [57]. This finding manifests the iron in the FeNi3@GCDs maybe a real active center.

XRD and TEM were carried out to further assess the crystalline phase and structure stability of FeNi3@GCDs-10 after long-time CP test. As presented in Fig. S5a, the XRD pattern still showed three main peaks at 44.2°, 51.5° and 75.8° of the FeNi3 alloy, indicating the electrocatalyst can keep better crystalline phase after undergoing long-time CP tests. Furthermore, several novel peaks can also been observed, where the typical peaks around at 21.2° and 36.6° were assigned to the FeOOH (PDF#81–0462), the clear shift in the XRD diffraction peaks of NiOOH (PDF#84–1459) can also be observed, being consistent with the results of XPS spectrum. Fig. S5b showed that the surface of electrocatalyst became rough and also generated lots of sheets, resulting from true active centers of FeOOH that produced during the electrolysis process, but the morphology was similar to the original one, indicating the as-prepared FeNi3@GCDs-10 was mechanically stable.

DFT calculations were carried out to illuminate the origin of the excellent electrocatalytic OER performance of the FeNi3@GCDs-10 dendrites. The FeNi3 (111) plane was selected as the theoretical model of the exposed surface for FeNi3 and FeNi3@GCDs-10 since the exposed (111) plane is found in the TEM image and PXRD pattern [58]. The optimal structures for different oxygen-containing adsorption on the FeNi3/FeNi3@GCDs-10 (111) were displayed in Fig. S6. The typical four sequential electron transfer steps of the OER process on FeNi3 and its composite were presented in Fig. 6
a, b. it demonstrated that the Ni sites at the set potentials of 0 V and 1.23 V possess strong adsorption, suggesting the Ni sites are not desirable active sites for the OER electrocatalysis. In contrast, in the FeNi3 model, due to the weak adsorption of *O, the potential barrier from *OH to *O is very large, while there is no potential barrier from *O to *OOH. The introduction of GCDs leads to the enhancement of the adsorption of *O and the decrease of the energy barrier from *OH to *O, while the energy barrier from *O to *OOH is not too high. The free energy of *O between *OH and *OOH was regulated to improve the catalytic activity. In order to gain a further insight into the role of GCDs on the catalytic performance of the FeNi3 system, the partial density of states (PDOS) of FeNi3 and FeNi3@GCDs were calculated to study their electronic structure (Fig. S7). Interestingly, the addition of GCDs can induce a slight variation of d-band of Fe and Ni near the Fermi level. Firstly, the d-band shape of FeNi3@GCDs become rougher, which is ascribed to the effect of GCDs on the different d orbitals of Fe and Ni (d Z2 , dx2-y2, dzx, dyz, dxy). In consideration of the key role of Fe in improving the OER performance of FeNi3@GCDs-10, we also calculate the PDOS of the d-orbit of Fe in detail (Fig. 6 c). However, by comparing the d-orbitals of Fe (on the surface of FeNi3, exposed on the surface of C- FeNi3, on the edge of C, coated with C), it was found that the d-bands of Fe after interacting with GCDs moves toward Fermi level = 0 eV, implying the effect of electronic structure regulation on FeNi3 induced by GCDs was not the key role in improving the OER performance. According to the differential charge density analysis (Fig. 6 d), it is disclosed that the charge of both the Fe and Ni sites could transfer to the C sites, and the C sites could also effectively strength the adsorption of *OH and *O. Therefore, the introduction of GCDs can induce the strong synergistic effect, which will significantly optimize the adsorption of intermediated species to promote the OER electrocatalysis.

According to our discussion couple with the results of experiments and DFT calculations, the enhanced mechanism of FeNi3@GCDs were conjectured. As showed in Fig. 7 , the GCDs played a key role in improving the electrocatalytic OER performance of FeNi3. On the one hand, the introduction of GCDs not only optimizes the morphology of FeNi3 to enlarge the electrochemical active areas, but also accelerates the charge transfer from both the Fe and Ni sites to the GCDs. On the other hand, GCDs can also enhance the adsorption energy of *O, benefiting for energy barrier from *OH to *OOH.

In summary, we have realized the simultaneous morphology and conductivity tuning of FeNi3 alloys to significantly improve their OER activity by introducing GCDs. Our findings suggest that the Fe sites in the FeNi3 alloys are the real active sites for OER electrocatalysts. More importantly, the incorporation of GCDs could induce strong synergistic effect and electronic effect to boost the electron transfer from Fe and Ni atoms to carbon. Furthermore, it is also demonstrated that GCDs can strengthen the adsorption of *O of Fe sites and lower the energy barrier for the formation of *O to optimize the free energy of *O. Benefitting from the high specific active areas, largely improved electrical conductivity, optimized free energy of *O, FeNi3@GCDs-10 can deliver remarkably high OER performance. This work has demonstrated the positive influences of the simultaneous morphology and conductivity engineering by GCDs on the OER performance, which will provide guidance for the fabrication more advanced OER electrocatalysts.

Zhao Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Xianli Xu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xinhua Lu: Visualization. Changchun He: Date curation, Supervision. Jingjing Huang: Investigation. Wei Sun: Investigation. Lin Tian: Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (20KJB150043), Xuzhou science and technology plan project of China (KC21294).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.01.088.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supports promote single-atom catalysts toward advanced electrocatalysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MOF-derived Hollow Heterostructures for Advanced Electrocatalysis
